summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software project failure is the norm rather than the exception, and that experienced programmers understand success requires avoiding almost everything going wrong rather than just doing a few things right. Drawing on Steve McConnell's research, he notes that roughly 25% of projects are canceled outright and another 50% go over budget or schedule. He uses the FBI's $170 million Virtual Case File disaster as a contemporary example of how even well-funded projects fail spectacularly. The post concludes that developers who have lived through failures become more cautious and effective precisely because they understand the pain they can inflict on users.
key_points: The difference between beginners and experienced programmers is that beginners think hard work leads to success, while veterans know hard work might only prevent failure
Software project success depends less on avoiding a few mistakes and more on doing almost everything right
Industry data suggests 25% of projects are canceled, 50% go over budget/schedule, and only 25% finish on time—often with reduced scope
The FBI's $170 million Virtual Case File project serves as a stark example of how even massive investments can result in complete failure
Developers who have experienced failure cycles work harder to understand pitfalls and should be trusted more than those who deny ever being part of disasters
money_quotes: New programmers think if they work hard, they might succeed. Experienced programmers know that if they work really hard, they might not fail.
Actually succeeding in a software project depends a whole lot less on not doing a few things wrong but on doing almost everything right.
A quarter of the projects are canceled before they're completed and another 50 percent are completed over budget or behind schedule.
The depressing reality of software development is that your project has to be almost supernaturally well run to avoid failure, much less succeed.
Only developers who have been through a number of failure cycles can appreciate the pain they're about to inflict on users.
I'd seriously question the credentials – or at least the intellectual honesty – of any developer who denied being a part of any software disasters.
key_insight: Software development success is not about doing things right—it's about the near-impossible task of avoiding the countless ways projects fail.
